response:
# Delete Project Confirmation Modal — Accessibility Testing Plan

> **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. This is a TESTING plan for an existing component. Review with a11y-critic after the test suite is implemented and passing.
> **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA
> **Users who need accessibility:** Keyboard-only users, screen reader users (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver), low-vision users
> **Assistive technologies:** NVDA (Windows + Firefox/Chrome), JAWS (Windows + Chrome), VoiceOver (macOS + Safari), keyboard-only

**Feature:** A confirmation modal that requires typing the project name to enable a destructive "Delete" button, dismissible via Delete, Cancel, Escape, or backdrop click — all of which restore focus to the trigger.
**Risk Level:** High (focus trap + focus restoration + destructive action + dynamic disabled-state; modal focus lifecycle is the single most common production a11y failure)
**Component Type:** Modal Dialog (APG Dialog Modal pattern) with confirmation-input sub-pattern

---

## Scope & Context

**What is being tested.** An existing React/TypeScript confirmation modal that gates project deletion. Engineering wants a repeatable accessibility test plan before the next sprint. The QA engineer executing this plan is fluent in Playwright but has not used screen readers, so every screen-reader step is scripted verbatim — what to press, what to listen for, and what counts as pass/fail.

**What user need this addresses.** Destructive actions must be confirmable and reversible-until-committed for *every* input modality. A keyboard or screen reader user must be able to (a) understand what the dialog is asking, (b) reach and operate the confirmation input and both buttons, (c) escape without committing, and (d) land back where they started. A focus that escapes to inert background content, or a focus that vanishes after close, silently breaks the entire flow for these users even when the mouse path works perfectly.

**Compliance target.** WCAG 2.2 AA. Success criteria exercised by this plan:
- **2.1.1 Keyboard** — all functionality operable by keyboard.
- **2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap** — focus can leave the dialog *via the dialog's own dismiss mechanisms* (Escape/Cancel/Delete), and Tab does not escape to the background. (The APG modal "trap" is a *cycle*, not a WCAG keyboard trap, precisely because Escape always provides an exit.)
- **2.4.3 Focus Order** — focus moves into the dialog on open and returns to the trigger on close.
- **2.4.7 Focus Visible** — every focusable element in the dialog shows a visible focus indicator.
- **2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Minimum)** — the focused element inside the dialog is not hidden behind the backdrop or sticky UI.
- **2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum)** — Delete/Cancel/close controls are at least 24×24 CSS px (AA); 44×44 is the AAA target we note but do not gate on.
- **1.3.1 Info and Relationships** — dialog title via `aria-labelledby`, description via `aria-describedby`, input label association, error/help association.
- **3.3.1 Error Identification / 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions** — the "type the project name" instruction is programmatically associated, and a mismatch is identifiable.
- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value** — `role="dialog"`, accessible name, and the Delete button's disabled state are exposed.
- **4.1.3 Status Messages** — any "deleting…" or success/failure status after committing is announced without moving focus.

**Assistive technologies under test.** NVDA + Firefox (primary, free, reproducible in CI-adjacent manual runs), JAWS + Chrome (enterprise Windows baseline), VoiceOver + Safari (macOS baseline). Rationale for pairings: screen readers are tuned to specific browsers; testing NVDA+Firefox, JAWS+Chrome, and VoiceOver+Safari covers the three highest-coverage real-world combinations and exercises the three different ways `inert` and dialog semantics get surfaced.

**Risk level: High.** Justification — three failure-prone behaviors compound here: (1) focus trap correctness, (2) focus restoration on four different exit paths, (3) a dynamically-toggled disabled state on a *destructive* control. Each is individually a common production miss; together they warrant the full lifecycle test matrix below rather than a static-state checklist.

**Constraints.**
- axe-core must report zero violations on every state variant before PR merge (CI gate).
- Playwright + axe-core already run in CI; new specs slot into that harness.
- The QA engineer has Playwright experience but no screen-reader experience — screen-reader steps are written as literal scripts, not "verify it's announced."

**Out of scope (negative space — what this plan does NOT cover).** This plan does not test the *backend* deletion (that the project is actually removed), undo/restore flows, multi-project bulk delete, the trigger button's own placement in the page, or non-AA AAA criteria beyond the noted target-size aspiration. It does not re-derive the component's design — it tests the behavior the fixture specifies. If the component's actual behavior diverges from the fixture (e.g., backdrop click does *not* close, or focus does *not* restore), that is a **finding to file**, not a test to silently rewrite.

---

## Pattern Grounding

**APG Dialog (Modal) pattern — the single authoritative reference for this entire plan:**
https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/dialog-modal/

Every test below maps back to a behavior this pattern specifies. The pattern's normative requirements we test:
- The element with `role="dialog"` has an accessible name (`aria-labelledby` → visible title) and, when a description is present, `aria-describedby` → the description paragraph.
- When the dialog opens, focus moves into it (the fixture moves it to the dialog container itself; see the note below on container focus).
- Tab and Shift+Tab cycle through the dialog's tabbable elements and **do not** move focus outside the dialog while it is open.
- Escape closes the dialog.
- When the dialog closes, focus returns to the element that invoked it (the trigger), unless context makes another location more logical.

**On `inert` vs `aria-modal`.** The fixture's component uses the `inert` attribute on background content. This is the *correct, modern* primary mechanism: `inert` removes background content from the tab order, from the accessibility tree's interaction surface, and from pointer events in one declarative step. We test `inert` as the primary mechanism. `aria-modal="true"` is a **compatibility fallback** for older AT that predates `inert` support — it tells the screen reader to confine virtual-cursor reading to the dialog subtree. We will **note** whether `aria-modal="true"` is present on the dialog and flag its absence only as a *compatibility advisory for legacy JAWS/older NVDA*, not as a required attribute. We do **not** prescribe `aria-modal` as mandatory, and we do not treat its absence as a failure when `inert` is correctly applied and the live AT matrix passes. (Belt-and-suspenders — `inert` on background *plus* `aria-modal` on dialog — is the most robust real-world configuration; if the team adopts both, all the better, but `inert` is what we gate on.)

**Note on focusing the dialog container.** The fixture moves focus to the `<div role="dialog">` itself on open (which requires `tabindex="-1"` on that container so it can receive programmatic focus). This is a legitimate APG-sanctioned variant: focusing the container causes the screen reader to announce the dialog's role and accessible name (and description, depending on AT) before the user tabs to the first control. The alternative pattern — focusing the first interactive element (here, the text input) — is also valid but announces the input first and the dialog framing second. Because the fixture specifies container focus, the announcement tests below assert the *container-focus* announcement order. If the live AT matrix reveals the description is **not** announced on container focus under one or more screen readers (a known inconsistency — `aria-describedby` on a focused container is read by some AT and not others), that is a finding with a concrete remediation (see Screen Reader section), not a silent pass.

---

## Test Environment Setup

| Layer | Tool | Where it runs | What it catches |
|-------|------|---------------|-----------------|
| Automated structural | axe-core (via `@axe-core/playwright`) | CI (every PR) | Missing/invalid ARIA, name/role/value gaps, contrast, label association, duplicate ids |
| Automated behavioral | Playwright (`@playwright/test`) | CI (every PR) | Focus trap cycle, focus restoration, `inert` presence, disabled-state transition, Escape/backdrop close |
| Manual keyboard | Real keyboard, no synthetic events | Local, per-release | Tab order sanity, focus-visible at each stop, real activation behavior |
| Manual screen reader | NVDA / JAWS / VoiceOver | Local, per-release + on dialog changes | Announcement content and order, description read, disabled-state announcement |
| Visual regression | Playwright screenshots (`toHaveScreenshot`) | CI (every PR) | Focus ring visibility, backdrop, invalid-input styling, enabled/disabled button styling |

**Critical Playwright rule for this component.** Use real keyboard events — `page.keyboard.press('Tab')`, `page.keyboard.press('Escape')` — never `element.focus()` to *simulate* tabbing and never JS-dispatched `KeyboardEvent`. Synthetic events bypass the browser's actual focus model and will give false passes on the focus-trap and restoration tests, which are the highest-risk behaviors here. Playwright's `keyboard.press` is CDP-backed and delivers genuine key events; that is the only acceptable mechanism for the trap/restore assertions.

**State variants the suite must cover** (each is a distinct axe scan + visual snapshot):
1. `modal-closed` — trigger button visible, no dialog in DOM (or `hidden`), background fully interactive.
2. `modal-open-empty` — dialog open, input empty, Delete button disabled.
3. `input-invalid` — input contains a non-matching value, Delete still disabled.
4. `delete-button-enabled` — input value exactly matches project name, Delete enabled.
5. (If the component shows a post-confirm state) `deleting` — Delete pressed, busy/status state.

---

## Test Suite Structure & Execution Order

Order is chosen so the cheapest, most-deterministic tests fail first and gate the expensive manual ones. Do not run screen-reader passes until automated structural + behavioral pass — a missing `aria-labelledby` should be caught by axe in seconds, not discovered 20 minutes into an NVDA session.

```
Phase 1  Automated structural (axe-core)        ─ blocks PR ─ run on all 5 state variants
Phase 2  Automated behavioral (Playwright)      ─ blocks PR ─ trap, restore, inert, disabled, Escape, backdrop
Phase 3  Manual keyboard walkthrough            ─ per release ─ confirms automated assumptions with human eyes
Phase 4  Manual screen reader (NVDA → JAWS → VO) ─ per release / on dialog change ─ announcement scripts
Phase 5  Visual regression                       ─ blocks PR ─ snapshot diffs on all variants
Phase 6  a11y-critic review checkpoint           ─ gate before merge ─ design-soundness review of results
```

Priority labels used below: **P0** = blocks merge; **P1** = blocks release; **P2** = tracked, fix within sprint.

---

## Phase 1 — Automated Structural Testing (axe-core)

**Goal:** Catch every machine-detectable ARIA/semantic/contrast defect on every state variant before a human spends a minute.

**What axe-core WILL catch here:** missing accessible name on the dialog, `aria-labelledby`/`aria-describedby` pointing at non-existent ids, input without an associated label, insufficient text/UI contrast, duplicate ids, invalid ARIA attribute values, button without discernible text.

**What axe-core WILL NOT catch (and therefore must be covered in Phases 2–4):** whether the focus trap actually cycles, whether focus restores to the trigger, whether `inert` is *functionally* removing background from tab order, whether the disabled-state change is *announced*, and whether the dialog announcement order is correct. axe validates structure, not the focus lifecycle.

**Method — run axe on each state variant.** For each of `modal-open-empty`, `input-invalid`, `delete-button-enabled` (and `deleting` if present), open the dialog into that state, then scan **scoped appropriately**:

- Scan the **dialog subtree** with `withTags(['wcag2a','wcag2aa','wcag22aa'])` and assert zero violations.
- Separately, scan the **full page** while the modal is open and confirm axe does not report the background controls as violations *and* (advisory) inspect that background interactive elements carry `inert`. axe will not fail on a correctly-inert background, but a full-page scan with the modal open is where a stray focusable background element (one the `inert` container missed) shows up as a contrast or name issue on something that should be unreachable.

**P0 assertions:**
- Zero axe violations on `modal-open-empty`, `input-invalid`, `delete-button-enabled`.
- The dialog's accessible name is non-empty and equals the visible title text "Delete Project: {name}" (axe `aria-labelledby` resolution).
- The text input has a programmatically associated, non-empty label.

**Example assertion shape (illustrative, not production code):**
```
results = await new AxeBuilder({ page })
  .include('[role="dialog"]')
  .withTags(['wcag2a','wcag2aa','wcag22aa'])
  .analyze();
expect(results.violations).toEqual([]);
```

**DOM verification requirement (do not skip).** axe passing is necessary but not sufficient. The behavioral phase MUST also assert, against the *rendered* DOM, that:
- `[role="dialog"]` has `aria-labelledby` whose value is the `id` of the visible title element, and that element exists and is non-empty.
- `[role="dialog"]` has `aria-describedby` whose value is the `id` of the description paragraph, and that element exists.
- The Delete button's `id` is referenced by nothing stale, and its disabled state is expressed in a way AT exposes (see Phase 2 disabled-state test for the `disabled` vs `aria-disabled` decision).

This guards against the classic failure where ARIA attributes are present but point at the wrong element or a duplicated id, which axe sometimes resolves leniently but AT does not.

---

## Phase 2 — Automated Behavioral Testing (Playwright)

This phase is the heart of the plan. It scripts the focus lifecycle that the fixture (correctly) calls out as the thing static tests miss.

### 2.1 — Focus moves INTO the dialog on open (P0)
WCAG 2.4.3.

**Steps:**
1. Render page with trigger button.
2. `page.keyboard` focus the trigger (Tab to it from a known start, or `trigger.focus()` is acceptable *here* because we are establishing precondition, not testing the trap).
3. Activate it with a real key: `await page.keyboard.press('Enter')` (and a sibling test with `Space`).
4. Assert the active element is the dialog container: `expect(page.locator(':focus')).toHaveAttribute('role','dialog')` (the fixture focuses the container; container must therefore have `tabindex="-1"` — assert that too).

**Pass:** focus is on `[role="dialog"]` immediately after open.
**Fail (file as P0 finding):** focus remains on the trigger, jumps to `<body>`, or lands somewhere other than the dialog/its first control.

### 2.2 — Focus trap cycles forward and does NOT escape (P0)
WCAG 2.1.1, 2.1.2 (cycle, not trap, because Escape exits), APG Dialog Modal.

This is the step-by-step keyboard script the fixture demands. The dialog's tabbable elements, in DOM/visual order, are: **[a] text input → [b] Cancel button → [c] Delete button** (Delete is reachable in the tab order even when disabled *only if* implemented with `aria-disabled`; if implemented with the native `disabled` attribute it is removed from the tab order — the test below branches on which is used, see 2.5). Assume for the forward-cycle test the input is filled so Delete is enabled and tabbable; also run the variant with Delete disabled to confirm the cycle still behaves.

**Forward-cycle steps (Delete enabled variant):**
1. Open dialog (focus on container).
2. `Tab` → assert `:focus` is the **text input** [a].
3. `Tab` → assert `:focus` is **Cancel** [b]. (If there is also a visible "X"/close control, insert it at its real position and assert it.)
4. `Tab` → assert `:focus` is **Delete** [c].
5. `Tab` → assert `:focus` cycles back to the **first tabbable element** [a, the input] — **and critically** assert it is NOT on any background element. Concretely: `expect(page.locator(':focus')).toBe(theInput)` AND `expect(backgroundTrigger).not.toBeFocused()`.

**Backward-cycle steps (Shift+Tab):**
6. From the input [a], `Shift+Tab` → assert `:focus` wraps to the **last tabbable element** [c, Delete].
7. `Shift+Tab` → Cancel [b]. `Shift+Tab` → input [a]. Confirm no background reached at any point.

**Escape-hatch confirmation (proves it is a cycle, not a WCAG trap):**
8. At any focus position, `page.keyboard.press('Escape')` closes the dialog (full test in 2.3). The existence of this exit is what makes the cycle WCAG-compliant under 2.1.2.

**Pass:** Tab from the last element returns to the first; Shift+Tab from the first returns to the last; the background trigger is never `:focus` while the dialog is open.
**Fail (P0):** any Tab/Shift+Tab lands focus on a background element, on `<body>`, or escapes the dialog.

**Why this scripting matters:** "verify focus stays in the modal" is untestable and unrepeatable. Asserting the exact element at each Tab stop, plus an explicit negative assertion that the background trigger is not focused after the wrap, is what catches the real bug (trap that wraps correctly forward but leaks on Shift+Tab, or a trap that includes a background element because the focusable-element query was scoped to `document` instead of the dialog).

### 2.3 — Escape key closes and restores focus (P0)
WCAG 2.1.2, 2.4.3, APG Dialog Modal.

**Steps:**
1. Open dialog from a known trigger.
2. Move focus somewhere inside (e.g., Tab to the input, type a few chars).
3. `await page.keyboard.press('Escape')`.
4. Assert the dialog is removed/hidden: `expect(page.locator('[role="dialog"]')).toBeHidden()` (or detached).
5. Assert focus is restored to the trigger: `expect(triggerButton).toBeFocused()`.

**Pass:** dialog closes AND `:focus` is the trigger.
**Fail (P0):** dialog closes but focus drops to `<body>` (the #1 production modal failure), or Escape does nothing.

### 2.4 — Backdrop click closes and restores focus (P0)
WCAG 2.4.3, fixture requirement.

**Steps:**
1. Open dialog.
2. Click the backdrop region *outside* the dialog panel: `await page.locator('.modal-backdrop').click({ position: { x: 5, y: 5 } })` (click near a corner to be sure it lands on backdrop, not the panel; or click coordinates known to be outside the panel bounding box).
3. Assert dialog hidden/detached.
4. Assert focus restored to trigger.

**Edge case to assert (P1):** a click that *starts* inside the panel and drags out (text selection) must NOT close the dialog. Test: `mouse.down()` inside the input, `mouse.move()` to backdrop, `mouse.up()` on backdrop → dialog stays open. This is a real, commonly-shipped bug (close handler keys off the up-target instead of the down-target).

**Pass:** backdrop click closes + restores focus; in-panel drag-to-backdrop does not close.
**Fail (P0 for the close+restore; P1 for the drag edge case):** as inverse.

### 2.5 — Conditional Delete button: disabled-state transition (P0)
WCAG 4.1.2; announcement aspect tested in Phase 4.

**First, determine the implementation and branch.** Inspect the rendered Delete button:
- **If it uses the native `disabled` attribute:** the button is removed from the tab order and from pointer events when disabled. axe is happy; screen readers announce "Delete, button, unavailable/dimmed" *when navigated to in browse mode* but the button is **not Tab-reachable** while disabled, so a Tab-only user cannot land on it to hear that it is disabled. This is acceptable behavior, but the *state change* (becoming enabled) is then communicated only by the button appearing in the tab order — which is fine, but the change itself is not actively announced.
- **If it uses `aria-disabled="true"` (with the button kept focusable):** the button stays in the tab order; a Tab user can focus it and hear "Delete, button, dimmed/unavailable"; and toggling `aria-disabled` to `false` changes what AT announces on next focus. The fixture's scope hints reference `aria-disabled="true"`, suggesting this approach. **If `aria-disabled` is used, the component MUST also prevent activation** (click/Enter must be no-ops while `aria-disabled="true"`) — assert that pressing Enter on an `aria-disabled` Delete button does NOT trigger deletion.

**Steps (covering both branches):**
1. Open dialog; input empty.
2. Assert Delete reflects disabled: either `expect(deleteBtn).toBeDisabled()` (native) OR `expect(deleteBtn).toHaveAttribute('aria-disabled','true')`.
3. Assert that while disabled, activating Delete does nothing: focus/click it (or press Enter when focusable) and assert no deletion side-effect / dialog still open.
4. Type a value that does NOT match the project name → assert Delete still disabled (and, Phase 4, that any "name doesn't match" help text is associated and—if it appears dynamically—announced via a live region).
5. Clear, then type the EXACT project name → assert Delete becomes enabled: `expect(deleteBtn).toBeEnabled()` OR `expect(deleteBtn).toHaveAttribute('aria-disabled','false')` (or attribute removed).
6. Assert Delete is now Tab-reachable and activatable.
7. Backspace one character → assert Delete returns to disabled (state is reactive in both directions, not one-way).

**Announcement of the transition (handed to Phase 4):** whether the enable/disable flip is *announced* depends on implementation. The most robust pattern is a **polite live region** (`role="status"` / `aria-live="polite"`) that states "Delete enabled — project name matches" when the match occurs, so a screen-reader user who is typing in the input hears confirmation without having to Tab to the button to discover it. Phase 4 scripts the listen-test; Phase 2 asserts the live region node exists and receives the expected text content on match.

**Pass:** disabled↔enabled tracks the input exactly in both directions; disabled Delete cannot fire; (if `aria-disabled`) activation is blocked.
**Fail (P0):** Delete enabled while text mismatches; disabled Delete still deletes; state only flips one direction.

### 2.6 — Cancel button closes and restores focus (P0)
WCAG 2.4.3, fixture requirement.

**Steps:** open → Tab to Cancel → `page.keyboard.press('Enter')` (and a `Space` variant) → assert dialog hidden → assert focus restored to trigger. Same pass/fail shape as Escape.

### 2.7 — Background `inert` is present and functional (P0)
WCAG 2.1.1, 2.4.3; this is the fixture's flagged "most commonly missed" test.

Two assertions — *attribute presence* and *functional unreachability* — because the attribute can be present on the wrong element, or absent on a stray container.

**Attribute presence:**
1. Open dialog.
2. Assert the background container(s) carry `inert`: `expect(page.locator('#app-root')).toHaveAttribute('inert','')` (or whichever element the component marks — read the component to identify it; do not assume the selector). If multiple sibling regions exist outside the dialog, assert each is inert or that a single common ancestor (excluding the dialog) is inert.
3. (Advisory, not gating) note whether the dialog itself carries `aria-modal="true"` as a legacy-AT fallback; record presence/absence in the report.

**Functional unreachability (the assertion that actually proves the user can't escape):**
4. With the dialog open and focus inside, attempt to Tab the entire cycle (from 2.2) and assert the background trigger and at least one other known background control are NEVER `:focus`.
5. Attempt to *click* a background interactive element through the backdrop: `await backgroundButton.click()` should either be intercepted by the backdrop or be a no-op due to `inert` — assert the background button's handler did not fire (e.g., a spy/flag, or assert no resulting navigation/state change). Note: clicking a backdrop will typically *close* the dialog (test 2.4); to isolate inert specifically, attempt a programmatic-position click on a background control that is NOT under the backdrop click-to-close zone if such exists, or assert via `inert` that pointer-events are disabled.

**On close, assert `inert` is REMOVED:**
6. Close the dialog (Escape) → assert the background container no longer has `inert`: `expect(page.locator('#app-root')).not.toHaveAttribute('inert')` → and assert a background control is focusable again. A dialog that forgets to remove `inert` on close leaves the *entire app* unusable to keyboard/AT users — assert the restore explicitly.

**Pass:** background is inert while open (attribute present, not Tab-reachable, background handlers don't fire), and inert is removed on close.
**Fail (P0):** background reachable while open, OR inert not removed on close.

---

## Phase 3 — Manual Keyboard Walkthrough

A human repeats the automated keyboard path to catch what assertions can miss (a focus ring that is technically present but visually invisible, a tab stop that is "focusable" but visually off-screen, a focus order that is technically a cycle but feels wrong). One pass per release; ~10 minutes.

| # | Action | Expected | WCAG | Pass/Fail |
|---|--------|----------|------|-----------|
| K1 | Tab to "Delete Project" trigger | Visible focus ring on trigger | 2.4.7 | |
| K2 | Press Enter | Dialog opens; focus visibly in dialog (on container/title region) | 2.4.3 | |
| K3 | Press Tab | Focus visibly on text input | 2.4.7 | |
| K4 | Type a non-matching value | Delete stays visibly disabled (dimmed) | 1.4.1, 4.1.2 | |
| K5 | Replace with exact project name | Delete becomes visibly enabled (style change is not color-only) | 1.4.1 | |
| K6 | Tab → Tab through Cancel/Delete | Each control shows a visible focus ring; ring not obscured by backdrop | 2.4.7, 2.4.11 | |
| K7 | Tab past last control | Focus wraps to first control, NOT to address bar/background | 2.1.2 | |
| K8 | Shift+Tab from first control | Focus wraps to last control | 2.1.2 | |
| K9 | Press Escape | Dialog closes; focus ring visibly back on trigger | 2.4.3 | |
| K10 | Reopen, Tab to Cancel, press Space | Dialog closes; focus on trigger | 2.4.3 | |
| K11 | Reopen, click backdrop | Dialog closes; focus on trigger | 2.4.3 | |
| K12 | Reopen, complete name, Tab to Delete, press Enter | Deletion proceeds; post-action focus is sensible (see note) | 2.4.3 | |

**K12 focus-after-commit note:** after a successful delete, the trigger button likely no longer exists (the project is gone). The component must place focus somewhere sensible — e.g., the project list heading, the next list item, or a confirmation status region — NOT `<body>`. If the plan author cannot confirm what "correct" post-delete focus is from the fixture, **flag it**: this is an under-specified behavior and a likely gap. Recommended target: move focus to the list container/heading and announce success via a polite live region.

**K4/K5 color-only check:** confirm the enabled/disabled distinction is conveyed by more than color alone (e.g., disabled also has reduced opacity + `disabled`/`aria-disabled` semantics, enabled has solid fill). WCAG 1.4.1.

---

## Phase 4 — Manual Screen Reader Testing

These are written as literal listen-scripts because the executing QA engineer has not used screen readers. Each row says exactly what to press and **what words to expect**, with an explicit pass condition. Run NVDA first (free, primary), then JAWS, then VoiceOver. Test on the dialog's open, the description read, the disabled→enabled flip, and each exit path.

**Universal setup per screen reader:**
- NVDA: launch NVDA, open the app in Firefox. Keep NVDA speech viewer open (Tools → Speech Viewer) so you can *read* what was spoken and paste it into the result log — this turns an audio test into a copy-pasteable evidence trail.
- JAWS: launch JAWS, open the app in Chrome. Use the JAWS speech history (INSERT+SPACE then H) to capture spoken text.
- VoiceOver: Cmd+F5 to start, open the app in Safari. Use the VoiceOver caption panel (VO menu → Captions) to read spoken output.

### 4.1 — Dialog open announcement (P0)
WCAG 4.1.2, 1.3.1. The fixture explicitly requires documenting *what NVDA announces* — role, title, description — and in what order.

| Step | Press | Expected announcement (NVDA + Firefox) | Pass condition |
|------|-------|----------------------------------------|----------------|
| SR1 | Tab to trigger, press Enter | NVDA announces the dialog: **"Delete Project: {name}, dialog"** — the accessible name (from `aria-labelledby`) followed by the role "dialog". | The project-name title AND the word "dialog" are both spoken when focus enters. |
| SR2 | (immediately after) listen for description | NVDA reads the description paragraph text referenced by `aria-describedby` — e.g., **"This action permanently deletes the project and cannot be undone."** | The description text is spoken on open. **If it is NOT** (a known AT inconsistency when `aria-describedby` is on a focused *container*), record as a finding — see remediation below. |
| SR3 | Press Tab | NVDA announces the input: its **label**, role "edit", and any associated instruction from `aria-describedby` on the input (e.g., "Type the project name to confirm"). | Label + "edit" + instruction all spoken. |

**JAWS (Chrome) expected (SR1–SR3 equivalent):** JAWS announces "Delete Project: {name}, dialog" on entry; description behavior matches NVDA's container-describedby caveat; on Tab to input, "Type the project name to confirm, edit, blank" or similar. Record actual.

**VoiceOver (Safari) expected:** VoiceOver announces "Delete Project: {name}, dialog" (VO tends to append "dialog" and may add "You are currently in a dialog"); on VO+Right or Tab to input, the label and "edit text" are spoken. Record actual.

**Remediation if description is not announced on container focus (concrete, not hand-wavy):** the most reliable fix is to ensure the description is announced as part of the input's context — associate the description with the *input* via `aria-describedby` (in addition to / instead of the dialog container), OR move initial focus to the first interactive element (the input) with the description associated to it, OR add the description text into the accessible name/description chain that is read on the first Tab. The point is: the user must hear *why this dialog is dangerous* before they can act — if container-focus doesn't deliver that under a target screen reader, the association must be repositioned so it does. File as a finding with this remediation attached; do not pass SR2 silently if the description is dropped.

### 4.2 — Reading the dialog in browse/virtual mode (P1)
Some users explore a dialog with the virtual cursor (NVDA arrow keys, VO+arrows) rather than Tab. Confirm `inert` confines them.

| Step | Press | Expected | Pass |
|------|-------|----------|------|
| SR4 | NVDA: Down Arrow repeatedly from dialog top | Reads title → description → input label → input → Cancel → Delete, then **does not** read any background page content | Virtual cursor cannot reach background text/controls while dialog is open (confirms `inert`/`aria-modal` confinement for the SR reading mode, not just the tab mode) |
| SR5 | VoiceOver: VO+Right repeatedly | Same confinement — VO cursor stays within the dialog | VO does not announce background content |

**This is the screen-reader counterpart to the Phase 2.7 inert test.** Phase 2 proves *Tab* can't escape; SR4/SR5 prove the *virtual cursor* can't escape. Both are required because `inert` handles tab-mode and (in modern AT) reading-mode, while older AT relies on `aria-modal` for reading-mode confinement — this step is exactly where a missing `aria-modal` fallback would surface on legacy JAWS. If SR4/SR5 leak to the background on an older AT version, that is the precise, evidence-backed justification for adding `aria-modal="true"` as a fallback.

### 4.3 — Delete button disabled→enabled announcement (P0)
WCAG 4.1.2, 4.1.3.

| Step | Press | Expected (NVDA) | Pass |
|------|-------|-----------------|------|
| SR6 | Tab to Delete while input empty/mismatched | NVDA: **"Delete, button, unavailable"** (or "dimmed") | The disabled state is spoken — the user knows Delete is not yet actionable |
| SR7 | Tab back to input, type the EXACT project name | If a polite live region is implemented: NVDA speaks the confirmation, e.g., **"Delete enabled — project name matches"** WITHOUT focus leaving the input | The match is announced while the user stays in the input (this is the good pattern) — OR, if no live region, see SR8 |
| SR8 | Tab to Delete again | NVDA: **"Delete, button"** (no longer "unavailable") | The state change is reflected on next focus even without a live region |

**Pass:** the disabled state is spoken on focus (SR6); after a match, either a live region announces it (SR7, preferred) or the button no longer reports unavailable on next focus (SR8, minimum). 
**Fail (P0):** Delete reports "button" (actionable) while the name does not match — a screen-reader user would believe they can delete when they cannot, or worse, the inverse where it stays "unavailable" after a valid match.

### 4.4 — Exit-path announcements (P1)
| Step | Press | Expected | Pass |
|------|-------|----------|------|
| SR9 | Escape | Dialog dismisses; NVDA announces the trigger button context where focus lands (e.g., "Delete Project, button") | Focus return is *audible* — user hears they are back on the trigger |
| SR10 | Reopen, activate Cancel | Same as SR9 | |
| SR11 | Complete name, activate Delete | Post-commit: a polite status announces success (e.g., "Project deleted") and focus lands on a sensible, announced location | Success is announced; focus is not lost to `<body>` |

### 4.5 — Screen-reader results log (mandatory evidence)
For each step SR1–SR11, paste the **actual** captured speech (from NVDA Speech Viewer / JAWS history / VO captions) next to the expected text. A screen-reader test without the captured-speech transcript is not evidence — it is an assertion. The log makes the test repeatable across team members and gives a11y-critic something concrete to evaluate.

---

## Phase 5 — Visual Regression Testing

Playwright `toHaveScreenshot` snapshots on each state variant; diffs block PR. The fixture names the required states.

| Snapshot | State | What it guards |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| `modal-closed.png` | Trigger visible, no dialog | Baseline; backdrop not bleeding through |
| `modal-open.png` | Dialog open, input empty, Delete disabled | Dialog layout, backdrop dimming, disabled Delete styling |
| `input-invalid-state.png` | Non-matching value typed | Any error/help styling is visible and not color-only |
| `delete-button-enabled.png` | Exact name typed, Delete enabled | Enabled Delete styling distinct from disabled by more than color |
| `focus-ring-input.png` | Tab on input | Focus indicator visible against dialog background (2.4.7) |
| `focus-ring-delete.png` | Tab on Delete | Focus indicator visible; not obscured by backdrop (2.4.7, 2.4.11) |

**Additional visual conditions (P1, run manually or as parametrized snapshots):**
- **200% browser zoom / 400% page zoom (1280px → 320px reflow):** the dialog reflows without horizontal scroll; both buttons remain reachable; the dialog is not clipped (WCAG 1.4.10, 1.4.4).
- **Windows High Contrast / forced-colors mode:** dialog border, focus ring, and disabled/enabled button distinction survive forced-colors (the disabled state must not rely on a removed background color alone). WCAG 1.4.11.
- **`prefers-reduced-motion`:** any open/close transition is reduced/removed. WCAG 2.3.3.

**Focus-indicator contrast (P0 within visual):** measure the focus ring against both the element and the dialog background — it must meet 3:1 (WCAG 2.4.7/1.4.11). A single-color ring that matches the dialog surface is invisible; if found, recommend the two-color (outline + box-shadow) technique. This is a finding, not just a snapshot.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

The component passes accessibility testing for this sprint when ALL of the following hold, with evidence attached:

**Automated (CI-gating, P0):**
- [ ] axe-core: zero violations on `modal-open-empty`, `input-invalid`, `delete-button-enabled` (and `deleting` if present).
- [ ] `[role="dialog"]` accessible name resolves to the visible title; `aria-describedby` resolves to an existing description element (DOM-verified, not just axe-passed).
- [ ] Focus moves into the dialog on open (2.1).
- [ ] Tab cycles forward to first; Shift+Tab cycles back to last; background trigger never focused (2.2).
- [ ] Escape closes + restores focus to trigger (2.3).
- [ ] Backdrop click closes + restores focus; in-panel drag-to-backdrop does not close (2.4).
- [ ] Delete disabled↔enabled tracks input exactly both directions; disabled Delete cannot fire (2.5).
- [ ] Cancel closes + restores focus (2.6).
- [ ] Background `inert` present while open, functionally unreachable (tab + click), and REMOVED on close (2.7).

**Manual keyboard (release-gating, P1):**
- [ ] Every tab stop shows a visible, non-obscured focus ring (K1–K12).
- [ ] Disabled/enabled Delete distinguished by more than color (K4/K5).
- [ ] Post-commit focus lands on a sensible location, not `<body>` (K12) — or the gap is filed if under-specified.

**Screen reader (release-gating, P0/P1):**
- [ ] On open, dialog role + title announced under NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver (SR1).
- [ ] Description is announced on open OR remediation applied and re-verified (SR2).
- [ ] Virtual-cursor reading is confined to the dialog under all three AT (SR4/SR5) — or `aria-modal` fallback added with evidence (legacy AT).
- [ ] Disabled state spoken on focus; enabled state reflected after match (SR6–SR8).
- [ ] Focus return is audible on Escape/Cancel; success announced after Delete (SR9–SR11).
- [ ] Captured-speech transcript attached for every SR step (4.5).

**Visual (CI-gating, P0/P1):**
- [ ] All named snapshots stable (Phase 5 table).
- [ ] Focus ring meets 3:1 against element and dialog background.
- [ ] Dialog reflows at 200%/400% zoom; survives forced-colors; honors reduced-motion.

**Definition of "below expectations" (auto-fail this plan's execution):** focus-trap "tested" by eyeballing without the per-stop assertions; no inert test; SR test that records "announced" without the transcript; missing focus-restoration assertion on any of the four exit paths; treating `aria-modal` as mandatory rather than testing `inert` as primary.

---

## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints

| Checkpoint | After Phase | What a11y-critic should scrutinize |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------------------------|
| 🔍 1 | Phase 2 complete (automated behavioral passing) | Are the focus-trap assertions *per-stop* (not "stays in modal")? Is there an explicit negative assertion that the background trigger is never focused? Are all FOUR exit paths (Escape, backdrop, Cancel, Delete) restoring focus, each separately asserted? Is the `inert`-removed-on-close assertion present? Is the disabled-state test branching correctly on `disabled` vs `aria-disabled`, and does it assert a disabled Delete cannot fire? |
| 🔍 2 | Phase 4 complete (screen reader run) | Does the SR log contain *captured speech*, not paraphrase? Is the open-announcement order (title → role → description) verified, not assumed? Was the description-on-container-focus caveat actually checked under each AT, with remediation applied and re-verified if it failed? Is virtual-cursor confinement (SR4/SR5) tested separately from tab confinement? Is the disabled→enabled announcement confirmed to not mislead (Delete never reports actionable while name mismatches)? |
| 🔍 3 | All phases + acceptance criteria | Is `inert` treated as the primary mechanism with `aria-modal` only as an evidence-backed legacy fallback (not prescribed blanket)? Are Cancel and Escape exit paths tested, not just the happy-path delete? Is post-commit focus handled (the under-specified gap)? Is the focus-ring contrast measured, not assumed? Any state variant missing an axe scan or a snapshot? |

**Escalation signal:** if checkpoint 🔍2 reveals that two or more screen readers drop the description, or that virtual-cursor confinement leaks, escalate to a **perspective-audit** focused on the screen-reader and keyboard-only perspectives before sign-off — the dialog framing and danger-comprehension are load-bearing for a destructive action, and a partial pass there is not acceptable for a delete confirmation.

---

## Failure Modes This Plan Is Designed to Catch

1. **Focus drops to `<body>` after close** — the single most common production modal failure. Caught by explicit `toBeFocused()` on the trigger across all four exit paths (2.3, 2.4, 2.6, K9–K12).
2. **`inert` not removed on close** — leaves the whole app unusable for keyboard/AT. Caught by 2.7 step 6.
3. **Trap leaks on Shift+Tab only** — forward cycle works, backward doesn't. Caught by 2.2 backward-cycle steps.
4. **Background reachable by virtual cursor despite Tab confinement** — `inert` missing or AT too old; `aria-modal` fallback absent. Caught by SR4/SR5.
5. **Description never announced** — `aria-describedby` on focused container dropped by AT; user never learns the action is irreversible. Caught by SR2 with remediation path.
6. **Delete enabled while name mismatches, or disabled Delete still fires** — destructive action with broken gating. Caught by 2.5.
7. **Drag-out text selection closes the dialog** — close keyed off the wrong mouse event. Caught by 2.4 edge case.
8. **Invisible focus ring on the dialog surface** — technically present, visually absent. Caught by focus-ring contrast measurement (Phase 5) and K6.
9. **Only the happy path tested** — Cancel/Escape ignored. Prevented structurally: every exit path is a separate P0 test.

---

### Contract Appendix (for spec-kitty-bridge translation)

### Architecture Overview
This is a verification plan for an existing modal dialog implementing the WAI-ARIA APG Dialog (Modal) pattern. Testing strategy is layered cheapest-deterministic-first: axe-core structural scans → Playwright behavioral (focus lifecycle) → manual keyboard → manual screen reader (NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver) → visual regression, gated by an a11y-critic review before merge. The focus lifecycle (open → trap-cycle → close → restore) is the central risk and gets per-stop assertions plus explicit negative assertions against background reachability. `inert` is tested as the primary background-isolation mechanism; `aria-modal` is an evidence-backed legacy fallback, not a prescribed requirement.

### Implementation Tasks

#### Task 1: axe-core structural scan suite (Phase 1)
Estimated Effort: low
Depends on: none

#### Task 2: Playwright behavioral suite — focus lifecycle (Phase 2: 2.1–2.7)
Estimated Effort: high
Depends on: Task 1

#### Task 3: Manual keyboard walkthrough checklist (Phase 3)
Estimated Effort: low
Depends on: Task 2

#### Task 4: Screen-reader listen-scripts + captured-speech log (Phase 4)
Estimated Effort: medium
Depends on: Task 2

#### Task 5: Visual regression snapshots + focus-ring contrast (Phase 5)
Estimated Effort: medium
Depends on: Task 1

#### Test Strategy for Task 1
Run `@axe-core/playwright` scoped to `[role="dialog"]` on each of `modal-open-empty`, `input-invalid`, `delete-button-enabled` (+`deleting` if present), tags `wcag2a/wcag2aa/wcag22aa`, assert zero violations; DOM-verify `aria-labelledby`/`aria-describedby`/label id resolution. WCAG 1.3.1, 4.1.2.

#### Test Strategy for Task 2
Real keyboard events only (`page.keyboard.press`, never synthetic). Assert: focus into dialog on open; per-stop Tab/Shift+Tab cycle with negative background-focus assertion; Escape/backdrop/Cancel/Delete each close + restore focus to trigger; disabled↔enabled transition both directions with disabled-cannot-fire; `inert` present-while-open / functionally-unreachable / removed-on-close. WCAG 2.1.1, 2.1.2, 2.4.3, 4.1.2.

#### Test Strategy for Task 3
Human executes the K1–K12 table once per release; logs pass/fail per row; confirms focus-ring visibility and non-color disabled/enabled distinction. WCAG 2.4.7, 2.4.11, 1.4.1.

#### Test Strategy for Task 4
Execute SR1–SR11 under NVDA+Firefox, JAWS+Chrome, VoiceOver+Safari; capture actual speech from each tool's caption/history; verify open announcement order (title→role→description), virtual-cursor confinement, disabled/enabled announcement, exit-path focus-return audibility. WCAG 4.1.2, 4.1.3, 1.3.1.

#### Test Strategy for Task 5
Playwright `toHaveScreenshot` on the six named states; manual/parametrized checks at 200%/400% zoom, forced-colors, reduced-motion; measure focus-ring contrast ≥3:1. WCAG 2.4.7, 1.4.10, 1.4.11, 2.3.3.

#### Acceptance Criteria for Task 1
Zero axe violations on all state variants; dialog name + description ids resolve to existing non-empty elements in rendered DOM.

#### Acceptance Criteria for Task 2
All P0 behavioral assertions green (2.1–2.7); background trigger never `:focus` while open; `inert` removed on close; disabled Delete cannot trigger deletion.

#### Acceptance Criteria for Task 3
K1–K12 all pass; every tab stop has a visible, non-obscured ring; disabled/enabled distinguished by more than color.

#### Acceptance Criteria for Task 4
Title+role announced on open under all three AT; description announced (or remediated + re-verified); virtual-cursor confined; disabled state never reports actionable on mismatch; captured-speech transcript attached for every step.

#### Acceptance Criteria for Task 5
All named snapshots stable; focus ring ≥3:1 against element and dialog background; dialog reflows at zoom, survives forced-colors, honors reduced-motion.

### Failure Modes
- Focus lost to `<body>` after any of the four exit paths (focus restoration miss — highest-frequency production failure).
- `inert` not removed on close (app-wide keyboard/AT lockout).
- Focus trap leaks on Shift+Tab or includes a background element (document-scoped focusable query instead of dialog-scoped).
- Virtual-cursor reaches background despite Tab confinement (missing `aria-modal` fallback on legacy AT).
- Description dropped by AT on container focus (danger context never communicated).
- Delete enabled on name mismatch, or `aria-disabled` Delete still fires.
- Backdrop close keyed off mouse-up target (drag-out selection closes dialog).
- Focus ring invisible against dialog surface (single-color ring matching background).
- Happy-path-only testing that skips Cancel/Escape exit paths.
